output:
**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

In federated self-supervised learning, where the absence of a label anchor leaves each client encoder defined only up to the SSL objective's symmetries (neuron permutations and feature-space rotations), is the cross-client divergence that FedAvg and L-DAWA combat in raw weight coordinates largely an artifact of coordinate-frame misalignment that can be removed by permutation/OT alignment of encoders before aggregation rather than merely dampened by layer-wise reweighting?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Federated self-supervised learning (F-SSL) is the practical route to turning the vast pools of unlabeled image data sitting on phones, cameras, vehicles, clinics, and other edge devices into a usable visual encoder without ever centralizing the raw data. The encoder produced by this pre-training stage is not an end in itself; it is the foundation that every downstream task inherits - recognition, detection, segmentation, face authentication, retrieval. (Improved self-supervised representations are application-agnostic; sensitive deployments such as face recognition or biometric authentication carry independent obligations around consent, demographic-bias auditing, and applicable regulation, beyond the scope of a methods paper.) That structural fact is exactly what makes the aggregation step so consequential. If the server-side aggregation that fuses client encoders each round silently degrades the learned representation, the damage is upstream and it compounds: every fine-tuned head, every linear probe, every deployed classifier is built on a weaker basis, and no amount of downstream engineering recovers what was destroyed during pre-training. The proposal here targets that root. It argues that a large fraction of what FedAvg and L-DAWA treat as genuine cross-client representational disagreement is not disagreement at all but coordinate-frame misalignment - the same representation expressed in a permuted (and, for rotation-tolerant objectives like SimCLR and BYOL, rotated) basis - and that aligning encoders into a common frame before averaging fixes the problem at its source rather than dampening its symptom.

The reason this is interesting beyond a single accuracy table is that it imports an entire body of machinery - symmetry-aware model fusion from the loss-landscape line of work (Git Re-Basin by Ainsworth et al., OT-Fusion by Singh and Jaggi, and the layer-matching ideas in FedMA and PFNM) - into federated representation learning, where it has never been deployed. Doing so reframes a concept the field currently takes as primitive. "Non-iid client drift," as measured by L-DAWA's layer-wise divergence between client and global weights, is computed in raw weight coordinates and therefore conflates two very different quantities: true representational divergence and the permutation/rotation freedom that the SSL objective leaves unconstrained. If the reframing is correct, then the community's standard way of measuring and treating client drift is partly measuring an artifact, and the correct pre-aggregation primitive is not "reweight the divergence" but "remove the removable frame, then aggregate the residual." That is a different default for the field, and it changes what future F-SSL methods should be compared against: any frame-blind reweighting rule is then competing inside a basis it never corrected.

The "fix the root, not the symptom" argument is sharpened by the source paper's own numbers. L-DAWA, FedU, and FedEMA all operate in weight space - they reweight or gate the contribution of divergent layers - but reweighting a misaligned average is still an average computed in the wrong basis. You can down-weight the noise but you cannot rotate the coordinate system by scaling its axes. This predicts a ceiling that frame-blind methods cannot pass, and L-DAWA's cross-device results are consistent with exactly such a ceiling: its gains there are tiny (on the order of roughly 0.25 to 1.26 percent), precisely the regime where many local steps between communications let each client's frame drift the furthest and where the missing label anchor makes the permutation freedom largest. A method that re-bases before aggregating predicts the opposite signature - the largest gains where divergence is most severe and most of that divergence is frame, not substance - which is both a stronger claim and a falsifiable one.

Practically, the payoff is concrete and budget-neutral. Alignment is a server-side operation on the model weights that already cross the wire; it adds no communication and no client-side burden, so operators obtain a better encoder under the SAME communication budget - the scarce resource in federated deployments. A stronger label-free encoder pre-trained on edge data directly improves privacy-preserving recognition, detection, segmentation, and authentication pipelines that fine-tune on top of it; we note that federated learning reduces but does not eliminate privacy risk - gradient/representation leakage and model-inversion remain feasible without differential privacy or secure aggregation, which are outside this proposal's scope. And the alignment itself yields a free diagnostic: the magnitude of the permutation/OT correction needed to bring clients into a common frame is a label-free, data-free measure of how severely Non-iid the federation is. Operators today have no clean way to gauge Non-iid severity without labels or shared probe data; the alignment cost provides one as a byproduct, usable for monitoring, client selection, or deciding when heavier personalization is warranted.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The proposal is easy to state but hard to execute, because every standard tool for "align two networks" was built for supervised classifiers on shared data, and federated SSL violates all three of those assumptions at once - it has heads and asymmetric branches instead of a clean classifier, it forbids sharing client data, and its symmetry group changes with the objective. Below are the five distinct obstacles, each with why the naive route fails and the concrete instrument that makes it tractable.

1. Matching SSL encoders with heads, asymmetric branches, layer-to-layer consistency, and per-round stability. Obstacle: Permutation/OT matching (Git Re-Basin, Singh-Jaggi) was derived for plain classifier stacks where you align hidden layers and the labeled output layer pins the final basis. SSL encoders have no label anchor and add a projector head (SimCLR, Barlow Twins) and a predictor head plus an EMA target branch (BYOL, SimSiam), so it is non-obvious WHAT to match and IN WHICH branch. A permutation applied to a conv layer must be propagated (composed) into the next layer's input channels, BatchNorm parameters, and residual shortcuts, or the re-based network computes a different function. Why naive fails: Matching each layer independently breaks functional equivalence - a permutation that is not composed forward corrupts the residual add and BatchNorm running statistics; matching the projector as if it were the encoder pollutes the part we actually aggregate; ignoring the EMA target branch in BYOL aligns the online net while leaving the target in a stale frame, so the two re-diverge next round. Why tractable: Functional-equivalence bookkeeping is mechanical and exact. We compute one permutation per matchable axis under the standard composition rule (apply P to a layer's output channels, P-transpose to the next layer's input channels, and the same P to that layer's BatchNorm gamma/beta/running-mean/var), handle residual blocks by forcing the shortcut and main path to share a permutation, and treat the projector as an optional, separately-matched module that is excluded from the encoder average by default. Stability across rounds is enforced by always matching each client to the CURRENT server frame (not to an arbitrary client) and carrying permutation bookkeeping forward, which keeps the global frame a slowly moving reference rather than a per-round random re-labeling.

2. Computing alignment with NO labels and NO shared client data. Obstacle: The cleanest alignment signal is activation correlation on a shared batch, but in F-SSL there is no public labeled set and, more importantly, raw client activations or data cannot leave the device. Privacy is the whole point of FL, so naive activation-matching is simply not allowed. Why naive fails: Activation-based matching requires either pushing a common probe batch through every client and returning per-neuron activations (leaks a fingerprint of client data and breaks the privacy contract) or sharing client samples (a non-starter). Either way it invalidates the federated setting we claim to operate in. Why tractable: Two admissible channels exist. Default is WEIGHT-ONLY matching (Git Re-Basin's weight-matching objective): the server already receives client weights, so aligning by maximizing summed inner products of matched weight rows uses information it legitimately holds and leaks nothing new. The optional sharper variant is a TINY server-side public unlabeled probe batch (e.g., a few hundred images from a disjoint public source such as a small ImageNet/STL unlabeled shard) pushed only through the weights the server already has - no client data and no client-side activation export. Both are privacy-clean; we lead with weight-only and treat the server-probe as an upper-bound ablation.

3. The objective-dependent symmetry group. Obstacle: The set of transformations that leave the SSL loss invariant differs by method. Contrastive/cosine objectives (SimCLR) and BYOL are invariant to feature-space ROTATIONS (orthogonal transforms of the embedding) in addition to neuron permutations; decorrelation objectives (Barlow Twins, VICReg) explicitly penalize off-diagonal cross-correlation and so are NOT rotation-invariant - they (nearly) pin the basis up to permutation and sign. A single fixed alignment group is therefore wrong for at least one family. Why naive fails: Permutation-only matching UNDERFITS SimCLR/BYOL, leaving a residual orthogonal misalignment that still inflates apparent divergence; conversely, adding a free orthogonal rotation for Barlow/VICReg OVERFITS - it can rotate into a basis the decorrelation loss did not intend, erasing the very structure that made those embeddings useful and hurting downstream probes. Why tractable: The symmetry group becomes a controlled ablation knob, not a guess. We run permutation-only as default for all methods, and add an orthogonal/rotation-alignment step (Procrustes/OT on the embedding axes) ONLY in the projector/embedding space and ONLY for the rotation-tolerant objectives (SimCLR, BYOL). The decorrelation methods are run permutation-only by design. The prediction "rotation helps SimCLR/BYOL but not Barlow" is itself a falsifiable test of the symmetry-group story, so the hard part doubles as evidence.

4. Cost, scaling, and multi-round convergence. Obstacle: Matching is not free and must run EVERY round across potentially many clients. Linear-assignment / Sinkhorn-OT per layer per client adds server compute; in cross-device settings with hundreds of clients per round the bill compounds. Worse, injecting a re-basing operation into the middle of an SSL optimization could destabilize training (oscillating frames, BatchNorm statistic mismatch) instead of helping. Why naive fails: Solving full OT at high cost every round for every client does not scale and may not even be necessary; and a re-basing step that changes the global frame discontinuously each round can fight the optimizer, causing loss spikes or representation collapse rather than the clean convergence we promise. Why tractable: The per-layer assignment is a small linear-assignment problem (Hungarian or a few Sinkhorn iterations on matrices the size of channel-count, e.g. 64-512), cheap relative to a round of local SSL training, and embarrassingly parallel across clients on the server. We bound cost further with the "align every k rounds" ablation. Stability is engineered: we always align to the persistent server frame (small, slowly-moving target), recompute BatchNorm statistics post-aggregation, and monitor cross-client effective rank to detect collapse early. Convergence is then an empirical claim we verify by showing the loss curves are no less stable than FedAvg under identical budgets.

5. Confound control - is it alignment or just regularization/noise? Obstacle: Any operation that perturbs and re-mixes client weights can incidentally act as a regularizer or noise injection that improves generalization for reasons unrelated to frame alignment. If align-then-aggregate wins, we must prove the win comes from REMOVING genuine coordinate-frame misalignment, not from a generic averaging-noise effect. Why naive fails: Reporting only "we beat L-DAWA" cannot distinguish the alignment mechanism from a lucky regularizer; reviewers (correctly) will not accept the mechanism claim from a headline accuracy number alone. Why tractable: Two placebos pin the mechanism. (i) Replace the OPTIMAL matching with a RANDOM (non-matched) permutation, the same operation applied to the same layers - if random "alignment" recovers the gain, the effect is regularization, not alignment, and the thesis fails. (ii) At iid (alpha -> large) the matching cost should approach zero, so align-then-aggregate must give essentially NO gain over FedAvg - any gain there also falsifies the thesis. Passing both, plus showing CKA and effective rank actually RISE after the optimal (not random) matching, ties the accuracy gain to the alignment mechanism specifically.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The cleanest way to see the gap is to line up the two families of prior work and show that neither one covers the target quadrant - label-free, multi-round, Non-iid alignment - and that the barrier was a seam between communities, not a solved problem. On the F-SSL aggregation side, L-DAWA (layer-wise divergence-aware weight aggregation), FedU, and FedEMA all act in weight space by measuring divergence between client and global parameters and then reweighting or gating each layer's contribution. The structural limitation is identical across all three: reweighting changes how much of each client you average, but it never changes the basis in which you average. If two clients have learned the same features under different neuron permutations or feature-space rotations, their weights are far apart in raw coordinates, and a reweighting rule can only dampen that apparent distance - it can never re-base one client onto the other. These methods therefore cannot, even in principle, remove permutation or rotation misalignment; they treat a coordinate artifact as if it were substantive disagreement and respond by averaging more cautiously in the wrong frame.

On the model-fusion side the alignment machinery exists but was built for a different setting. Git Re-Basin (Ainsworth et al.) and OT-Fusion (Singh and Jaggi) establish that two independently trained networks typically live in the same loss basin up to a neuron permutation, and that matching that permutation before merging removes most of the apparent barrier between them; FedMA and PFNM bring layer-wise matching into federated learning. But this entire line was developed for SUPERVISED networks and predominantly for ONE-SHOT or single-merge fusion, where a label-anchored objective already pins down most of the frame and the merge happens once. F-SSL is exactly the regime these methods never addressed and exactly where alignment should matter most: with no label anchor, the SSL objective's symmetry group is at its largest (permutations always, plus feature-space rotations for SimCLR/BYOL-style objectives), so the permutation freedom between clients is greatest - and because F-SSL is multi-round, the frames drift apart again after every local epoch, so a one-shot re-basin does not directly transfer. Nobody connected the two observations: Git Re-Basin and OT-Fusion say independently trained nets differ by a permutation; F-SSL says Non-iid clients diverge; no one asked whether the F-SSL divergence is largely just the missing permutation, applied per round.

A third family - FedCA and Orchestra - does pursue cross-client representation consistency, so it is important to say precisely where we go beyond them rather than claim a vacuum. FedCA aligns representations through a SHARED dictionary built from shared/public data, and Orchestra enforces consistency through global clustering coordinated across clients. Both achieve alignment, but they buy it with shared data or shared cluster structure, which carries a privacy cost and an extra coordination channel. Our wedge aligns through weight and representation GEOMETRY alone - the permutation/OT correspondence is computed from the model parameters that already reach the server - so it needs no shared dictionary, no public probe set, and no global clustering. That is a cleaner and strictly more privacy-preserving alignment story, and it occupies a different mechanism than FedCA/Orchestra rather than merely tuning theirs.

The barriers that kept this quadrant open are concrete, which is why the naive route looked blocked. First, the two communities do not cite each other: the loss-landscape-symmetry/model-fusion literature and the F-SSL-aggregation literature evolved in parallel with little contact, so the question was never framed. Second, SSL architectures complicate "what to match" - projector and predictor heads, and the asymmetric EMA-target branches in BYOL and SimSiam, mean it is non-obvious which sub-networks should be brought into correspondence and which symmetries are actually free. Third, the multi-round nature of F-SSL means frames re-drift every communication round, so a single re-basin does not solve it; alignment must be re-applied per round, which the one-shot fusion work never had to confront. Fourth, the most powerful matching technique - activation-based correspondence - appears to require shared data to compute activations on, which federated constraints forbid, making the obvious path look impossible. What is genuinely NEW here is therefore twofold and both heads must stay visible: (1) the OPERATOR - a label-free, no-shared-data, multi-round, per-round alignment-before-aggregation step for F-SSL encoders, matching via weight/representation geometry rather than shared activations; and (2) the REFRAMING - recasting L-DAWA-style weight-space divergence as partly a removable coordinate-frame artifact rather than pure representational disagreement. Critically, the claim is falsifiable: a random-permutation placebo (align to a random matching instead of the matched one) should recover none of the gain, and the benefit should fall toward zero as data approaches iid, since with little Non-iid drift there is little frame misalignment to remove. If those predictions failed, the reframing would be wrong - which is exactly what separates a positioned novelty claim from an asserted one.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

The method is a server-side align-then-aggregate operator that re-bases every client encoder into a common frame each round before averaging, leaving the client-side SSL training untouched. It is a drop-in replacement for the aggregation step, so it composes with existing baselines (including L-DAWA's reweighting). Everything below is sized for academic compute (ResNet-18 default, CIFAR/Tiny-ImageNet/STL-10 scale). Consistent with a privacy-preserving framing, only trained weights are transmitted and no raw client data or client-side activations leave the device; we also note that federated learning reduces but does not eliminate privacy risk - gradient/representation leakage and model-inversion remain feasible without differential privacy or secure aggregation, which are outside this proposal's scope.

1. The align-then-aggregate server operator (per round). a. Receive each participating client's encoder weights (and, optionally, projector head). No client activations or data are received. b. For each client, compute a layer-wise neuron-permutation matching AGAINST THE CURRENT SERVER ENCODER (the persistent global frame), not against another client. Default solver is weight-matching a la Git Re-Basin: choose per-layer permutations that maximize the summed inner product between matched client and server weight rows, propagating each permutation forward under the composition rule (output-channel P, next-layer input-channel P-transpose, and the same P applied to BatchNorm gamma/beta/running-mean/var); residual blocks force shared permutations on the main and shortcut paths. The optional sharper variant uses a tiny server-side probe batch, which must be public, non-sensitive data; only trained weights are transmitted, and no raw client data or client-side activations leave the device. c. Variant (ablation): replace the linear-assignment weight-matching with OT/Sinkhorn neuron alignment a la Singh-Jaggi (soft transport plan, entropic regularization, a few Sinkhorn iterations), which generalizes hard matching to soft fusion. d. Optional orthogonal-rotation alignment: for rotation-tolerant objectives (SimCLR, BYOL), add a Procrustes/orthogonal alignment in the projector/embedding space only. Decorrelation objectives (Barlow Twins) skip this by design. e. Apply the matching to re-base each client into the server frame. f. Aggregate the re-based clients TWO ways for comparison: (i) plain weight average (the clean test of "align then FedAvg"), and (ii) a drop-in combination where L-DAWA's per-layer divergence reweighting is applied AFTER re-basing (to show alignment and reweighting are orthogonal/complementary rather than competing). g. Stability bookkeeping: carry the forward/inverse permutations so the global frame evolves smoothly across rounds; recompute BatchNorm running statistics post-aggregation; the BYOL EMA target branch is re-based with the same permutation as its online network so the two stay in one frame.

2. Datasets and Non-iid sweep. CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, Tiny-ImageNet (200 classes, 64x64), and STL-10 (includes a large unlabeled split well suited to SSL). Non-iid partitions are drawn with a Dirichlet distribution over classes with alpha in {0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, iid}; alpha = 0.1 is most heterogeneous, iid is the homogeneous control. Linear-probe/kNN evaluation uses the standard labeled test sets; SSL pre-training uses only images.

3. Architectures and SSL methods. Encoder: ResNet-18 default, with a single ResNet-50 scale check to confirm the operator survives wider/deeper channels. SSL methods: SimCLR (contrastive, rotation-tolerant), Barlow Twins (decorrelation, rotation-pinning), and BYOL (asymmetric predictor + EMA target, the stress test for branch consistency). Standard projector (and BYOL predictor) MLP heads.

4. FL regimes. Cross-silo: few clients with more data each (e.g., 5-10 clients, full participation, more local epochs per round) - mirrors the L-DAWA cross-silo setting we must reproduce. Cross-device: many clients with little data each (e.g., 100+ clients, partial participation, 1-2 local epochs per round). Both run for a fixed total round/compute budget held IDENTICAL across all methods so comparisons are honest.

5. Baselines (reproduced under identical budgets). FedAvg, Loss-based aggregation, FedU, and L-DAWA as the headline target; optionally Orchestra. We reproduce L-DAWA exactly so the reference numbers (cross-silo SimCLR+Barlow: FedAvg 77.46/52.11, Loss 77.52/52.68, FedU 76.70/52.25, L-DAWA 81.87/57.81 on CIFAR-10/100) are matched before any of our deltas are reported.

6. Metrics. Primary (downstream quality): linear-probe top-1 and kNN top-1 on the frozen pre-trained encoder. Because Non-iid client distributions can encode demographic or population skew, we also report per-client linear-probe accuracy as a fairness-relevant axis alongside aggregate metrics. Mechanism metrics: (a) cross-client CKA on a shared, server-side public unlabeled probe set measured BEFORE vs AFTER alignment, computed through the uploaded weights with no client-side activations exported (alignment should raise it); (b) cross-client and aggregated effective/stable rank of representations, to test the collapse-preservation claim (alignment should preserve or raise effective rank rather than averaging it away); (c) permutation/OT matching cost as a function of Dirichlet alpha - the label-free Non-iid-severity diagnostic. We also report communication overhead (alignment is weight-only, so client->server payload is unchanged) and server-compute overhead (added linear-assignment/Sinkhorn time per round).

7. Ablations. (a) Alignment group: permutation-only vs permutation+orthogonal/OT, per objective. (b) Alignment frequency: every round vs every k rounds (cost/benefit). (c) Match with vs without the projector head included. (d) Per-objective behavior: SimCLR vs Barlow Twins vs BYOL, to test the symmetry-group prediction (rotation step helps SimCLR/BYOL, not Barlow).

8. Placebo / falsification tests (explicit pass/fail). (i) Random-permutation placebo: replace the OPTIMAL matching with a RANDOM (non-matched) permutation, the same operation applied to the same layers. PASS if the gain collapses to baseline (mechanism is real alignment); FAIL the thesis if random "alignment" reproduces the gain (effect was generic regularization). (ii) iid no-gain test: at iid (alpha -> large) matching cost should approach zero, so align-then-aggregate must give ~no gain over FedAvg. PASS if the gain vanishes at iid; FAIL the thesis if a real gain persists there.

Expected outcomes. If the thesis holds, align-then-aggregate (even with a plain post-alignment average) beats L-DAWA at small alpha (e.g., alpha = 0.1, 0.2) on linear-probe and kNN top-1, and the margin GROWS as alpha shrinks (more heterogeneity -> more removable frame misalignment -> more headroom). Cross-client CKA and effective/stable rank rise measurably after the optimal matching but not after the random placebo. Matching cost increases monotonically as alpha decreases, validating it as a label-free Non-iid-severity diagnostic. The random-permutation placebo yields no gain, and the iid setting yields essentially no gain, both as required. Combining alignment with L-DAWA's reweighting gives an additional, mostly additive bump, demonstrating the two mechanisms are complementary (alignment fixes the basis; reweighting then dampens residual true disagreement). The honest negative result that would refute the thesis: if at small alpha plain FedAvg-after-alignment does NOT exceed FedAvg/L-DAWA, OR the random-permutation placebo recovers the same gain, OR a real gain appears at iid, then cross-client divergence in F-SSL is NOT largely frame misalignment, and the contribution reduces to a (still-useful but much weaker) diagnostic rather than a superior aggregator. Throughout, we treat the resulting encoders as application-agnostic artifacts and flag that responsible downstream use - especially in biometric or other sensitive settings - remains the deploying party's obligation beyond the scope of this methods work.
